source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.6 sudo (pp. 299-301)

questions.q: What is the role of sudo?
options: To let root assign specific commands (normally root-only) to ordinary users to run
To switch permanently to the root account
questions.why: sudo delegates chosen system commands to ordinary users without giving them the full root account.

questions.q: Edit the sudoers file safely (the recommended command, which edits /etc/sudoers).
questions.why: visudo edits /etc/sudoers with syntax checking on save - safer than editing the file directly.

questions.q: Check /etc/sudoers for syntax errors without editing it.
questions.why: visudo -c validates the sudoers syntax - a bad sudoers file can lock everyone out of sudo.

questions.q: In the sudoers line 'root ALL=(ALL) ALL', what do the four parts mean?
options: user, the machines allowed, the identities that can be assumed, the permitted commands
questions.why: Format is 'user MACHINE=(run-as) COMMANDS' - who, on which hosts, as whom, running what.

questions.q: How do you write a sudoers rule that applies to a GROUP rather than a single user?
questions.why: A leading % denotes a group, e.g. %wheel ALL=(ALL) ALL.

questions.q: As an ordinary user, list the sudo commands available to you.
questions.why: sudo -l shows the commands the current user is permitted to run via sudo.

questions.q: How must the authorized commands be written in a sudoers rule?
options: As absolute paths (e.g. /sbin/shutdown); multiple commands are comma-separated
questions.why: Commands are given by absolute path (comma-separated for several); naming /sbin/shutdown lets the user run it with any options.
